claim_chain: B cells switch from quiescence to proliferation upon activation
Proteomic analysis reveals metabolic reprogramming including upregulation of amino acid transporters and cholesterol biosynthetic enzymes
SLC7A5 is markedly upregulated and its deletion impairs B cell proliferation
LPS elevates intracellular cholesterol and inhibition of HMG-CoA reductase blocks proliferation
The effect is mediated by dual requirement for cholesterol metabolism and protein prenylation
This metabolic rewiring is not unique to TLR4 but observed across multiple activation pathways

typical_pattern: Opening statement of finding from proteomic or functional data
Description of specific proteins or pathways identified
Comparison to previous literature or related cell types
Functional validation with genetic or pharmacological perturbation
Mechanistic interpretation linking observation to broader biological process

likely_priorities: Mechanistic clarity distinguishing cholesterol versus prenylation effects
Validation across multiple B cell activation pathways
Comparison to T cell metabolism and identification of B cell-specific features
Functional consequences beyond proliferation such as antibody production
In vivo relevance of ex vivo findings
likely_attacks: Statins have pleiotropic effects beyond cholesterol that complicate interpretation
The relative contribution of LDL uptake versus de novo synthesis is context-dependent
Regulation of SLC7A5 may differ between B and T cells
Ex vivo stimulation conditions may not reflect physiological activation
Feedback mechanisms complicate interpretation of inhibitor studies
reviewer_preference_profile.inference_basis: The paper explicitly acknowledges that 'Interpretation of the effects of statins on B cells is, however, complex' because statins affect not only cholesterol but also prenylation and ubiquinone pathways. It notes that 'The precise balance between these two mechanisms in B cells may depend on the duration of stimulation and the availability of LDL in the extracellular environment.' The authors compare their findings to T cells multiple times, noting differences such as 'transcriptional profiling following LPS stimulation of B cells has suggested that Slc7a5 mRNA induction in response to LPS can occur in the absence of c-Myc, suggesting that the regulation of SLC7A5 may not be identical between T and B cells.' These passages indicate the authors are defending against concerns about mechanistic complexity, cell-type specificity, and context-dependence.

The precise balance between these two mechanisms in B cells may depend on the duration of stimulation and the availability of LDL in the extracellular environment

verification_status.notes: The article provides substantial discussion and abstract text with clear mechanistic findings, functional validation, and appropriate hedging around complex interpretations. All extracted phrases are literal substrings from the input. No external references, invented numbers, or author names were included.
